title: Soda farl
subtitle: Irish griddle bread, four-quarter flatbread of soda dough
excerpt: Soda farl is a four-quarter Irish flatbread made from soda bread dough, cooked on a dry griddle rather than baked in the oven. It is a breakfast staple in Northern Ireland, cooked fresh on the morning, served split with butter or as part of an Ulster fry.
sourceNotes: Soda farl (from the Irish 'fardel', meaning quarter) is a Northern Irish griddle bread documented in regional Irish domestic cookery from the nineteenth century. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), records similar griddle-baked soda breads from the border counties. The recipe is a variant of Irish soda bread (bicarbonate of soda, buttermilk, soft flour) divided into quarters and cooked on a cast-iron griddle rather than in the oven. The technique is common to several flatbread traditions across the British Isles where griddle-cooking predated the domestic oven.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely, wrap individually, freeze. Reheat in a dry pan for 3 minutes each side or toast from frozen.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily; cook two rounds on two griddles simultaneously.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten the day they are cooked. Can be refreshed in a dry pan for 2 minutes the next morning.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Cooked on a medium griddle or cast-iron frying pan. No oil. Medium heat is critical: too hot and the outside burns before the interior cooks through; too low and the farl stays dense.

glossaryTerms.definition: Soda farl dough is shaped into a round roughly 2 cm thick, then divided into four quarters with a knife before going on the griddle. The name 'farl' comes from the old Irish and Scottish word for a quarter or fourth part. Each farl cooks with the cut edges exposed, which allows steam to escape and the bread to cook through without a doughy centre.

content.text: Soda farl is the quickest bread you can make. From mixing bowl to plate in under 30 minutes, no yeast, no proving. The dough is the same as soda bread but handled differently: shaped into a round, cut into the traditional

, and cooked on a medium griddle. The bread cooks through by a combination of the griddle heat and the steam trapped inside the dough as the surface sets. Split and buttered while still warm, it has a crumb that is somewhere between a soda bread and an English muffin.

attrs.prepNote: or soft Irish flour if available

content.text: Mix the flour, bicarbonate of soda, and salt in a large bowl. Make a well in the centre and pour in the buttermilk. Stir quickly with a knife until the dough just comes together into a rough, slightly sticky mass. Do not overwork — 10 strokes at most. Turn out onto a lightly floured surface and pat gently into a round approximately 18 cm across and 2 cm thick. Cut into four equal quarters with a single decisive stroke of a knife.

Heat a griddle pan or heavy cast-iron frying pan over medium heat, without oil. When a pinch of flour dropped on the surface turns pale gold within 20 seconds, the griddle is ready. Lay the four farls cut-edge down on the griddle, spaced slightly apart, and cook for 8 to 10 minutes on the first side until the underside is evenly browned and the edges are beginning to set. Flip onto the flat sides and cook for 5 minutes more on each flat face, turning twice, until all sides are browned and the bread feels light when you tap the base. The interior should read 90°C on a probe.
Serve warm. Split down the cut edge with your thumbs rather than cutting — the crumb tears apart cleanly. Butter both halves while the steam still rises from the cut surface.
